title: Lecture Notes: Introduction to Shiny Apps
section: title: “Lecture Notes: Introduction to Shiny Apps”
text: title: “Lecture Notes: Introduction to Shiny Apps”

title: Lecture Notes: Introduction to Shiny Apps
text:
What is a Shiny App?
Shiny is an R package that helps to build interactive web applications directly from the R code. It allows data teams to share data insights with non-technical stakeholders without requiring front of the app web development skills.

title: Lecture Notes: Introduction to Shiny Apps
text:
Architecture: Client-Server Model
A traditional Shiny app runs on a two-part client-server framework: 1. User Interface (UI): The web page running in the user’s local browser that collects what the user inputs, this can be dropdown selections, to then display the rendered outputs. 2. Server Logic: The backend of the engine. When users change an input, the web page transmits that data to a running R server. The server then runs the necessary calculations in R and gives the updated visuals to the browser.

title: Lecture Notes: Introduction to Shiny Apps
text:
Reactivity
The core driver behind Shiny is reactive programming. Shiny automatically keeps track of relationships between inputs and outputs, kepping only the specific R expressions that need updating when a user changes a setting.

title: Deep Dive: Shinylive (Serverless Apps)
text: Shinylive allows for interactive Shiny applications to run entirely serverless inside a regular web browser.

title: Deep Dive: Shinylive (Serverless Apps)
text: Shinylive allows for interactive Shiny applications to run entirely serverless inside a regular web browser.

title: Deep Dive: Shinylive (Serverless Apps)
text:
How it Works: WebAssembly (Wasm)
Shinylive influence WebAssembly (Wasm) to compile the R programming language runtime into instructions that a browser can execute directly. When a user loads your site, the lightweight R environment is cached right in their browser, meaning all reactive computations happen locally on their computer.

title: Deep Dive: Shinylive (Serverless Apps)
text:
Comparison Table








Feature
Traditional Shiny
Shinylive




Hosting Needs
Dedicated R Server
Static web hosting (GitHub Pages, Quarto-Pub)


Computation Location
Cloud / Remote Server
User’s local browser


Cost & Scalability
Paid plans required for heavy usage
Completely free; infinitely scalable


Initial Load Time
Instant
Slightly slower (loads R runtime first)

title: Deep Dive: Shinylive (Serverless Apps)
text:
Pros and Cons of Shinylive

Pros

Zero Hosting Costs: Deploy full apps easily on static, free services like GitHub Pages.
Infinite Scale: The host site will never crash from high concurrent traffic because every user’s device does its own heavy lifting.
Data Security: Data processing remains only inside the user’s browser without having to transfer to an external server.



Cons

Initial Download Overhead: The initial page load takes a few seconds longer to grab the browser-side R runtime.
Hardware Constraints: Complex calculations can be limitted by the speed of the user’s personal device rather than the speed of the cloud servers.
